course_description:
This course develops intermediate skills in creative music making music using music technologies and digital audio workstations. Topics include audio and MIDI recording, editing and mixing techniques, sound design, mix automation and critical listening.

Please note that this course offering may be cancelled if fewer than 10 students enrol.

This course guides students to create and share music using technology. Students develop knowledge and skills to creatively produce music, including foundational understandings, recording, sampling, synthesis, mixing, and releasing. Though weekly in-person lectures, lab tutorials, online content, and independent practice, students will have opportunities to create, collaborate, and develop skills to create music in their own style and in their own way.

Focused in-class Practical Project assessments develop particular skills in short focused activities, while a Major Project allows students to pursue their own musical interests more freely through the independent creation of a piece of music. A final Release Plan assessment offers students the opportunity to consider how they might realistically finalise, distribute and promote the music they've made for their Major Project.

Students who have completed MUSC1010 (Introduction to Music Technology)ﾠ can expect to build on the sound design, sampling, production, and mixing skills they have already acquired by delving deeper into more advanced tools, knowledge, and techniques.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
restrictions: Course offering may be cancelled unless a minimum of 10 students enrol.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
This course has been designed with the expectation of in-person attendance at lectures and tutorials as well as individual practice outside class time. The best way to experience this course, develop your skills and creativity, and to be part of the creative community of your classmates is to turn up.
course_aims: This course aims to empower students to create and share music using technology. Students gain knowledge and skills to creatively produce music, including foundational understandings, recording, sampling, synthesis, mixing, and releasing. Upon completion of the course, students will have completed a substantial individual musical work, and create a realistic plan to commercially release it.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand and apply practical music production techniques including recording, sampling, synthesis, and mixing, combing Digital Audio Workstation software with hardware such as microphones, audio interfaces, and controllers.
Understand core conceptual and theoretical concepts that relate to the recording and manipulation of sound.
Work creatively with others by collaborating, listening, sharing, and providing and receiving peer feedback.
Navigate the conception, production, and release of a substantial independent creative project.
Understand current industry practices related to releasing, distributing, and promoting music.

assessment_summary.title: Practical Projects
assessment_summary.due_date: Practical Project 1 9/03/2026 - 13/03/2026; Practical Project 2 23/03/2026 - 27/03/2026; Practical Project 3 20/04/2026 - 24/04/2026; Must be completed during the scheduled Laboratory Session as per the timetable provided.

assessment_summary.title: Major Project
assessment_summary.due_date: Draft Feedback Session 27/04/2026 - 1/05/2026; Final Project Submission 22/05/2026 - 22/05/2026

assessment_details.title: Practical Projects
assessment_details.due_date: Practical Project 1 9/03/2026 - 13/03/2026 Practical Project 2 23/03/2026 - 27/03/2026 Practical Project 3 20/04/2026 - 24/04/2026 Must be completed during the scheduled Laboratory Session as per the timetable provided.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Practical Projects are in-class tasks which focus on a particular aspect of music production. They are designed to encourage practical learning whilst also assessing developing skills. Practical Projects are short, focused, and contained, and are designed to develop the skills necessary for completing the final Major Production Project. Practical Projects are completed in regular class time with assistance from the teacher. At the beginning of class, students will be provided with the information or files necessary to complete the task, and must complete and submit before the end of class. See Blackboard for details on this assessment task. All the knowledge and skills required to complete the task will be provided in advance via tutorials and lectures, and students will have an opportunity to practice the skills both in-class and independently. PLEASE NOTE: GENERATIVE AI This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission will occur during scheduled class time, according to submission instructions provided on Blackboard.
Students must complete course tasks using the prescribed software and hardware.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension requests may be submitted up to 24hrs after the assessment is due. If an extension is granted, students are responsible for liaising with course staff to ensure a suitable time to re-sit the assessment, with consideration to the availability of staff and lab equipment.
Late submission
Absence during an in-class assessment without medical or other documentation will result in a score of 0 for the missed project. If a project is missed for legitimate personal or medical reasons, and documentation is provided, an extension can be granted. In this case, it is the student's responsibility to apply for an extension and liaise with the tutor to arrange a suitable time to re-sit the task.

assessment_details.late_submission: Absence during an in-class assessment without medical or other documentation will result in a score of 0 for the missed project. In this case, it is the student's responsibility to apply for an extension and liaise with the tutor to arrange a suitable time to re-sit the task.

assessment_details.title: Major Project
assessment_details.due_date: Draft Feedback Session 27/04/2026 - 1/05/2026 Final Project Submission 22/05/2026 - 22/05/2026
assessment_details.task_description: The Major Project is a significant creative work, created individually outside of class time. Within some prescribed parameters (e.g. duration, minimum number of tracks, etc), it allows students flexibility to explore genres, techniques, and ideas which are of personal interest. The major project is an opportunity for students to apply knowledge and skills learnt throughout the semester within an individually meaningful musical context. The Major Project is due at 5pm on Friday of Week 12 (22/05/2026), with a compulsory draft feedback session during the Week 9 tutorial. Draft Feedback Session Students will present a significant work-in-progress draft to their tutorial class in Week 9, and will receive feedback from the tutor and peers. Presentation of the draft and participation in the feedback session is a pass/fail hurdle requirement for the Major Project Assessment Item. That means, your draft is not graded, but you must present and participate in order to pass the Major Project item as a whole. Final Project Submission Students will submit their final project online on Friday 22/05.2026 by 5pm. Submission must include: Exported WAV file of the final track All working project files Incremental backups showing progress A project info sheet including information about sample sources, techniques, and the creative process (template provided on Blackboard). Students will be required to keep incremental backups of their work-in-progress, and document any use of third-party samples, third party plug-ins, or musical source material that is not completely original. Detailed information about the Major Project will be posted on Blackboard. PLEASE NOTE: GENERATIVE AI This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed technical understanding of their production work independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Draft must be presented in class in Week 9.
Final Project must be submitted online including exported WAV, all project files, periodic backups, and completed project information template.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Release Plan
assessment_details.other_conditions: Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Create an 800-word written release plan for the commercial/public release of your Major Project work. Using the template provided, draw on lecture content, readings, and your own research related to current music industry practices and music release strategies to create a clear, concise, and actionable plan for release. Because the work you produced for the Major Project is unique, your release strategy will also be unique. It will be tailored to the style, context, and strengths of your Major Project work, and to your own developing identity as an artist. Your release plan must be: realistic - taking advantage of current and local opportunities, using a budget of no more than $800. practical - clear and detailed enough to guide day-to-day work on the release. The overarching strategy is digested into actionable tasks. holistic - including plans for mastering, administration, disctribution, and promotion of the release. PLEASE NOTE: GENERATIVE AI This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.
learning_resources.other_course_materials:
If we've listed something under further requirement, you'll need to provide your own. Required Item Description Further Requirement Portable USB Storage or Reliable Cloud Storage You will produce work each week in tutorials and will need to save and backup your files. Files stored locally on lab computers are inaccessible for other workstations and are periodically deleted.
You'll need at minimum a reliable 16GB USB stick, or ideally a good quality portable hard drive with 500GB capacity or more.
Cloud storage may also be used, provided you have sufficient space, can readily enter your username and password at the start of every session, and remember to logout at the end of every session. own item needed Studio Monitoring Headphones Wired over-ear studio monitor headphones (not wireless-only) are required for music production. These can range in cost from $75 to $750.
We do not recommend students rush this purchase; we can provide headphones during lab classes until you have your own. Good headphones are an excellent investment in your production practice, take the time to try a few and get the right thing for your budget and requirements. Ask your tutor for advice if you're not sure. own item needed 1/4" Headphone Adapter Whether you're using your own headphones or using school-owned headphones, a 1/4" headphone adapter is required to connect to lab equipment. These are essential to bring to every class and can be purchased at JB Hifi or StoreDJ. own item needed Recommended Item Description Further Requirement Dan Hosken, An Introduction to Music Technology (New York: Routledge, 2011). electronic resource David Miles Huber & Robert E. Runstein. Modern Recording Techniques. 7th ed. Oxford: Focal, 2009. UQ library electronic resource Andrea Pejrolo, Creative Sequencing Techniques for Music Production (Second Edition), (Focal Press, Boston, 2011). UQ library electronic resource Mike Senior, Mixing Secrets for the Small Studio (Boston, Focal Press, 2011). UQ library electronic resource Snoman, Rick (2013) ‘Chapter 6: Sampling and Sample Manipulation’ from Dance Music Manual: Tools, toys and techniques, Second Edition. Burlington, MA: Focal Press. D’Escriván, Julio (2011) ‘Chapter 8: The studio as experimental lab’ from Cambridge Introductions to Music: Music Technology. Gibson, David (2005) ‘Chapter 2: Visual Representations of “Imaging”’ from The Art of Mixing: A Visual Guide to Recording, Engineering, and Production. Boston, MA: Course PTR. Dean, Tamara (2009) ‘Chapter 15: Sampling’ from Composition for Computer Musicians. Boston, MA: Course PTR. Alten, Stanley R. (2011) ‘Chapter 14: Sound Design’ from Recording and Producing Audio for Media. Boston: Course Technology / Cengage Learning. Pejrolo, Andrea (2011) ‘Chapter 1 – Setting Up Your Creative Environment: The Studio’ from Creative Sequencing Techniques for Music Production, Second Edition. Oxford: Elsevier. Russ, Martin (2008) ‘Chapter 1: Background’ from Sound Synthesis and Sampling, Third Edition. Burlington, MA: Focal Press. Izhak, Roey (2013) ‘Compressors’ from Mixing Audio: Concepts, Practices and Tools, Second Edition. Oxford: Focal Press. Savage, Steve. "Building a Mix" from Mixing and Mastering in the Box: The Guide to Making Great Mixes and Final Masters on Your Computer, Oxford University Press, 2014

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Lecture Weekly lectures cover key concepts and demonstrate techniques.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Tutorial Weekly tutorials focus on practical skills, demonstrations, collaboration, and discussion. Tutorials are a good opportunity for hands-on practice and one-to-one guidance from staff.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
